title: How Do You Actually Spot an Anomaly?
concept: Detecting an anomaly means picking a measurable sense of 'distance from normal' and setting a threshold — cross it, and a point gets flagged.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does anomaly detection flag, and does it need labeled examples to do it?

options.text: Data points that break the pattern the rest of the data follows, learned with no labels required
Only transactions that a human has already manually labeled as fraud
Groups of similar points, the same way clustering does

exercises.explain: Right! Anomaly detection learns what 'normal' looks like from the data itself, then flags what doesn't fit — no labels needed to get started.

cards.title: The fish out of water
cards.body: You'd spot a fish flopping around on a sidewalk in half a second — it's obviously not where fish belong. But "obviously wrong" is a feeling. To teach a computer the same instinct, you need to turn that feeling into a number.

cards.title: What actually makes something an anomaly?
cards.body: A point counts as an anomaly when it's far from normal in some measurable sense — far from the average, far from the usual spread, or far from every other point nearby. Pick a way to measure "far," set a threshold, and anything past it gets flagged.

cards.title: A feeling becomes a thermometer reading
cards.body: "That kid seems warm" is a hunch. "101.4°F, and normal is 98.6°F" is a measurement you can act on. Anomaly detection does the same swap: it turns "this data point feels off" into a specific number you can threshold and automate.

cards.title: Different rulers for "far"
cards.body: "Far" isn't always the same kind of distance. A transaction amount can be far from the average spend. A sensor reading can be far from the usual range. A user's behavior can be far from every other user's nearest behavior. Same idea, different rulers — you'll meet several of them in this unit.

cards.title: "Far" doesn't mean far away on a map
cards.body: People hear "distance" and picture physical space. In anomaly detection, distance is just a number describing difference — degrees from a set temperature, dollars from a typical purchase, standard deviations from a mean. None of it has to involve geography.

cards.title: Normal vs. Far
cards.body: The core question every anomaly detector asks about a point:

left.text:
Small measured distance
Matches the usual pattern
Not flagged

right.text:
Large measured distance
Crosses the threshold
Flagged for review

exercises.prompt: What is the key idea that turns "this looks weird" into something a computer can actually check?

options.text: Measuring how far the point is from normal using some numeric distance, then comparing that number to a threshold
Asking a human to eyeball every single data point
Deleting any point that a person dislikes

exercises.explain: Exactly! A measurable distance plus a threshold is what makes "weird" checkable by a machine.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In anomaly detection, "distance" always means physical or geographic distance.
exercises.explain: False! Distance here just means a numeric measure of difference — like dollars from a typical purchase or degrees from a normal temperature.

exercises.sentence: A point is treated as an anomaly when it is ___ from normal by some measurable amount.
exercises.explain: Anomaly detection flags points that sit far from normal on whatever ruler you choose to measure them with.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best shows a "feeling" being turned into a measurable anomaly signal?

options.text: A nurse converts "this patient looks feverish" into "101.4°F, which is 2.8 degrees above the 98.6°F normal"
A nurse writes "patient seemed a bit off today" in a private note
A nurse guesses the patient's temperature without taking a reading

exercises.explain: Correct! Turning a hunch into an actual measured distance from normal is exactly what makes it checkable and automatable.

exercises.prompt: A ride-share app notices one driver's trip route deviates far from every historical route between the same pickup and drop-off points. A teammate says, "That's not an anomaly, nothing physically broke." Why is this still a textbook anomaly detection case?

options.text: Anomaly detection only requires a measurable distance from normal — here, the route's distance from typical routes — not physical breakage
It isn't anomaly detection unless a law was broken
Only numeric sensor data like temperature can ever be used for anomaly detection
Routes can never be compared to each other mathematically

exercises.explain: Exactly! Anomaly detection just needs some measurable sense of 'far from normal' — a route's shape compared to typical routes counts just as much as a sensor reading does.
